headline: A Vulture Returns: Cambodia's Conservation Victory—and Its Cost
meta_description: After a decade away, a critically endangered white-rumped vulture has returned to Cambodia's Lomphat Wildlife Sanctuary—but mining and dams threaten the ecosys…
summary_short: Ten years after vanishing, a white-rumped vulture has returned to Cambodia's Lomphat Wildlife Sanctuary. The sighting shows conservation works—but industrial threats loo…
body:
A white-rumped vulture landed at a feeding station in Cambodia's Lomphat Wildlife Sanctuary this June, marking its first return in a decade. The moment felt like vindication for the teams working there. Surveyors spotted the critically endangered bird at a "vulture restaurant" run by NatureLife Cambodia during routine census work. The same count also revealed five red-headed vultures, including two juveniles—evidence that breeding programs are succeeding.

"The return of the white-rumped vulture shows that our conservation efforts are making a difference," said Bou Vorsak, CEO of NatureLife Cambodia.

But the sanctuary's success story masks a darker reality. The 356,087-hectare preserve faces simultaneous assault from industrial development. Mining companies continue quarrying marble in conservation zones despite expired licenses. Meanwhile, the proposed Lower Srepok 3 hydropower dam—a 376-megawatt project—threatens to flood core sanctuary areas and submerge the Veal Kambor Community Protected Area where local residents have traditional resource rights.

Since 2020, Cambodia's government has granted at least five mining concessions in adjacent community-protected areas. Local families report losing rice fields and forest access with minimal compensation. The vulture's return, then, represents not a triumph complete, but a race against time—conservation gains hanging by a thread as bulldozers rumble closer.
